refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safety). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified design parameter, not part of the FR itself. The FR now resides purely in the problem domain—what capability must exist—without prescribing how latency will be achieved or what specific threshold defines 'safe.'
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator command output) under agreed network conditions. Confirm that measured latency permits tele-operator to execute representative safe remote operations (e.g., controlled movement, emergency stop response) without safety incidents or operator-reported inability to control the equipment safely.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs from the remote station to the equipment with measurable latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state feedback (sensor data, status) from the equipment to the remote station with measurable latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall support tele-operator perception and decision-making for safe remote operation of the equipment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, encoding, transmission, decoding, and actuation stages, with per-stage latency targets that sum to ≤150 ms p95.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel architecture (primary high-bandwidth channel for video/sensor streams; secondary low-latency control channel for operator commands) with independent buffering and prioritization strategies to decouple real-time control from bulk data transfer.

DP3: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) management that dynamically adjusts encoding parameters, frame rates, and transmission redundancy based on network condition monitoring, maintaining latency bounds under variable network states.

DP4: Implement a state-synchronization model with predictive local feedback at the operator console (e.g., echo of operator input, local physics simulation) to mask network latency perception while maintaining safety-critical state consistency with the remote site.

DP5: Use a centralized latency monitoring and telemetry system that continuously measures end-to-end latency, detects excursions beyond the 150 ms threshold, and triggers graceful degradation or operator alerts before safety is compromised.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 (Dual-Channel Architecture) + DP3 (Adaptive QoS) + DP5 (Latency Monitoring), integrated as a composite solution. DP2 provides logical separation of concerns (control vs. bulk data), DP3 ensures robustness under variable network conditions, and DP5 provides observability and safety guardrails. DP1 is a prerequisite design discipline (latency budgeting) that applies across all candidates. DP4 is a complementary UX/safety enhancement but not the primary latency solution.

potential_sources: DP2 and DP3 are tightly coupled: the dual-channel design assumes QoS differentiation is available; if the network substrate does not support priority queuing or separate virtual circuits, the independence of channels is compromised.
DP5 (monitoring) depends on bidirectional telemetry channels; if the primary communication path is unidirectional or heavily asymmetric, latency measurement fidelity may degrade.

Implicit coupling between DP2 (dual channels) and the operator interface: the console must present unified control semantics despite dual underlying channels; poor abstraction here creates hidden state inconsistency.
DP3 (adaptive QoS) introduces feedback loops that can oscillate if tuning parameters are not carefully chosen; coupling between network condition detection and encoding adjustment must be damped.
coupling_check.notes: The selected composite DP exhibits acceptable loose coupling if each channel (control and data) is independently designed with clear interface contracts and if QoS adaptation is decoupled from the core control loop via a separate monitoring/adjustment subsystem. Risk is elevated if network substrate does not natively support QoS differentiation. Mitigation required before proceeding to DP->IM mapping.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: The agreed network conditions are stable and characterized (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profile). RISK: If network conditions are poorly specified or highly variable, latency targets may be unachievable; recommend explicit network SLA definition.

ASSUMPTION: The tele-operator's cognitive and reaction time is compatible with 150 ms latency (i.e., the operator can safely perceive and respond within this window). RISK: If the task requires sub-100 ms closed-loop control (e.g., precision manipulation), 150 ms may be insufficient; recommend human factors validation.

ASSUMPTION: Dual-channel architecture can be realized without significant cost or complexity overhead. RISK: If the deployment environment (e.g., constrained bandwidth, single network interface) does not support dual channels, this DP becomes infeasible.

ASSUMPTION: Adaptive QoS mechanisms (frame rate reduction, encoding quality adjustment) are acceptable to the operator and do not degrade situational awareness below safe thresholds. RISK: Aggressive quality reduction may hide critical visual cues; recommend operator feedback and safety validation.

ASSUMPTION: Latency monitoring can be implemented with sufficient accuracy and does not itself introduce significant latency. RISK: Measurement overhead or clock synchronization errors could mask true latency or create false alarms.

ASSUMPTION: The worksite safety plan and operational policies are compatible with remote operation at 150 ms latency (e.g., no requirement for sub-100 ms emergency stops). RISK: If safety policies mandate tighter latency, the FR acceptance criteria must be revised upward.

ASSUMPTION: The remote site and operator console have sufficient computational resources to support encoding/decoding, QoS adaptation, and monitoring without introducing additional latency. RISK: Resource contention or thermal throttling could violate latency bounds under peak load.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by a tele-operator, subject to: (1) p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions (specify bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); (2) graceful degradation of video/sensor quality (not control latency) when network conditions degrade; (3) continuous latency monitoring with operator alert if p95 exceeds 140 ms (10 ms safety margin); (4) human factors validation confirming that 150 ms latency is compatible with the intended task and operator cognitive load; (5) integration with worksite safety plan to ensure no safety-critical operation requires sub-100 ms closed-loop control. DP selection: Composite of DP2 (dual-channel architecture with independent control and data streams), DP3 (adaptive QoS with frame rate and encoding adjustment), and DP5 (continuous latency telemetry and operator feedback). Defer DP4 (predictive local feedback) to a secondary enhancement phase pending operator acceptance testing. Coupling mitigation: Establish explicit interface contracts between DP2 channels and DP3 QoS adaptation; decouple DP3 feedback loops from core control via dedicated monitoring subsystem; validate network substrate QoS capabilities before IM realization.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates dual-channel command/telemetry separation (DP2), adaptive QoS negotiation and enforcement (DP3), and latency monitoring with safety thresholds (DP5). Owns the real-time control loop, network abstraction layer, and observability instrumentation for remote operation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission parameters; Teleoperation module enforces latency budgets and QoS constraints on ingress.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Streams vehicle state, latency metrics, and QoS status to upstream consumers; adaptive sampling and prioritization applied within Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety-critical alerts when latency thresholds are exceeded or QoS degrades below safe operating bounds; owned by Teleoperation's monitoring subsystem.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network abstraction; Teleoperation manages dual-channel routing (control vs. bulk), QoS tagging, and link-layer monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP2 (dual-channel) is native to teleoperation architecture (command vs. telemetry separation). DP3 (adaptive QoS) is a core concern for remote operation under variable network. DP5 (latency monitoring) is essential for safety-critical teleoperation. Minimal diffusion: latency budgeting discipline (DP1) applies globally but is enforced here; UX enhancements (DP4) are orthogonal. Network Link interface is owned; SafetyAlert and Telemetry are clear boundaries.

Moderate fit. DP5 (latency monitoring and safety guardrails) aligns with Safety Services' mandate, but DP2 (dual-channel architecture) and DP3 (adaptive QoS) are network/transport concerns that would diffuse into Teleoperation anyway. Safety Services would become a consumer of latency metrics rather than owner of the full composite DP. High coupling risk: Safety Services would depend on Teleoperation for QoS and channel management, creating circular dependencies.

Poor fit. Vehicle module owns actuators and sensors, not network control or QoS adaptation. DP2, DP3, and DP5 are all teleoperation-layer concerns that would be orphaned or duplicated if placed here. Severe diffusion: command routing, QoS negotiation, and latency monitoring would leak into Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing the composite DP here would avoid clear ownership and accountability. DP2, DP3, and DP5 would still need to be realized within Teleoperation or Safety Services, creating ambiguity. High diffusion: no clear interface boundaries or module cohesion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the composite DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) because it is the architectural layer responsible for bridging operator intent and vehicle state across a variable, latency-prone network. Dual-channel separation (DP2) is a core teleoperation pattern; adaptive QoS (DP3) is essential for maintaining safety and responsiveness under network stress; latency monitoring (DP5) is the observability mechanism that enables safe operation. All three DPs are cohesive within the teleoperation domain and would diffuse significantly if split across other modules.
containment_risks: Risk: Latency budgeting discipline (DP1) must be enforced globally across Vehicle, Safety Services, and Teleoperation. Teleoperation owns the monitoring and enforcement, but Vehicle and Safety Services must respect the budgets. Mitigation: DP1 is a design discipline, not a module; it is applied as a constraint across all modules, not contained within one.

Risk: Safety Services may need real-time access to latency metrics to make safety decisions (e.g., failsafe triggers). If Teleoperation owns DP5 (latency monitoring), SafetyAlert interface must be low-latency and reliable. Mitigation: SafetyAlert is an explicit external interface; Teleoperation publishes metrics synchronously.

Risk: Adaptive QoS (DP3) may require coordination with Vehicle-level resource constraints (e.g., sensor sampling rates, actuator bandwidth). Teleoperation must not assume unlimited capacity. Mitigation: Network Link interface abstracts vehicle-side constraints; Teleoperation negotiates QoS within advertised limits.

Risk: Dual-channel architecture (DP2) may require hardware support (e.g., dual network interfaces, separate control/data paths). If hardware is not available, DP2 may degrade to logical separation. Mitigation: DP2 is a design pattern; physical realization is a separate concern (IM layer detail).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The composite DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) is well-aligned with the Teleoperation module boundary. However, clarify that DP1 (latency budgeting) is a cross-cutting design discipline that applies to all modules, not a contained DP. If latency monitoring (DP5) must be shared with Safety Services, consider introducing an explicit 'Latency Observability' interface (derived from Telemetry) that Teleoperation publishes and Safety Services consumes, rather than moving DP5 ownership. This preserves cohesion in Teleoperation while enabling safety-critical latency awareness.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable latency constraint (safe remote operation) with explicit acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the real-time control loop, network abstraction, and latency monitoring subsystem, making end-to-end latency observable and controllable. SafetyAlert interface provides explicit verification hook for safety guardrails. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that must be validated during integration, but the module boundary is clear and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR is allocated to a single, well-defined IM (Teleoperation) with clear ownership of DP2 (dual-channel), DP3 (adaptive QoS), and DP5 (latency monitoring). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) establish unambiguous responsibility boundaries. No orphaned or ambiguous allocations detected. Cross-cutting concern (DP1: latency budgeting discipline) is correctly identified as a design constraint applied across modules, not a contained DP.

diffusion_risks: DP1 (Latency Budgeting Discipline): Not contained within Teleoperation IM; applies globally. Mitigation is correct—this is a design discipline, not a module responsibility. However, Teleoperation must enforce budgets at ingress (Tasking interface) and egress (Telemetry interface). Verify that budget enforcement logic is not duplicated in Vehicle or Safety Services.

DP5 (Latency Monitoring) Diffusion into Safety Services: Containment score of 0.92 for Teleoperation is justified, but SafetyAlert interface creates a dependency chain. If Safety Services requires sub-millisecond latency metrics for failsafe decisions, Teleoperation's monitoring subsystem must guarantee synchronous publication. Risk: Latency monitoring logic could leak into Safety Services if safety thresholds are not clearly defined at the interface boundary. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert contract explicitly (metric types, update frequency, reliability guarantees).

DP3 (Adaptive QoS) Coordination with Vehicle Constraints: Teleoperation owns QoS negotiation, but Vehicle may have resource constraints (sensor sampling, actuator bandwidth) that are not visible at the Teleoperation layer. Risk: QoS adaptation could oscillate or fail if Vehicle-side limits are not communicated. Mitigation: Network Link interface must include a capability advertisement mechanism (e.g., max sampling rate, available bandwidth) that Teleoperation queries during QoS negotiation.

DP2 (Dual-Channel Architecture) Hardware Dependency: Logical separation is owned by Teleoperation, but physical realization (dual network interfaces, separate control/data paths) may be constrained by hardware availability. Risk: If hardware does not support dual channels, DP2 degrades to logical separation, potentially increasing latency. Mitigation: Document DP2 realization assumptions (hardware vs. logical); if hardware is unavailable, re-evaluate latency budget feasibility.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation IM achieves 0.92 containment score with minimal diffusion (0.08). All three primary DPs (DP2, DP3, DP5) are cohesive within the teleoperation domain and would fragment significantly if distributed. DP1 is correctly identified as a cross-cutting design discipline, not a contained DP. DP4 (UX/safety enhancements) is orthogonal and does not compete for ownership. No circular dependencies detected. SafetyAlert interface creates a clear consumer relationship with Safety Services, avoiding diffusion. Recommendation: Maintain Teleoperation as the single owner; do not split DP2, DP3, or DP5 across modules. Introduce explicit 'Latency Observability' interface (derived from Telemetry) if Safety Services requires real-time metric access, rather than moving DP5 ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The composite DP (DP2 + DP3 + DP5) directly addresses the FR requirement for 'end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.' DP2 (dual-channel architecture) reduces latency by separating control and bulk data, ensuring control commands are not blocked by telemetry streams. DP3 (adaptive QoS) maintains responsiveness under variable network conditions by prioritizing control traffic. DP5 (latency monitoring) provides observability and safety guardrails to enforce the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion. DP1 (latency budgeting) is a prerequisite discipline that ensures all components respect the latency budget. The composite DP is necessary and sufficient to satisfy the FR. No gaps or contradictions detected. DP4 is correctly identified as complementary but not primary to latency reduction.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP1 (Latency Budgeting Discipline) as a cross-cutting design constraint, not a contained DP. Document the latency budget allocation across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services modules. Specify enforcement points (e.g., Tasking ingress, Telemetry egress, SafetyAlert publication).

Define SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly: metric types (latency, QoS status), update frequency, reliability guarantees (e.g., at-least-once delivery), and latency thresholds that trigger alerts. This prevents diffusion of DP5 logic into Safety Services.

Introduce an explicit 'Latency Observability' interface (derived from Telemetry) that Teleoperation publishes for Safety Services consumption. This preserves DP5 ownership in Teleoperation while enabling safety-critical latency awareness without circular dependencies.

Document DP2 (Dual-Channel Architecture) realization assumptions: specify whether dual channels are physical (separate network interfaces) or logical (traffic prioritization on a single link). If hardware constraints prevent physical dual channels, re-evaluate latency budget feasibility and consider alternative DP candidates.

Add a capability advertisement mechanism to the Network Link interface: Vehicle must communicate max sampling rate, available bandwidth, and QoS constraints to Teleoperation. Teleoperation uses this information to negotiate adaptive QoS (DP3) within feasible bounds.

Verify that the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is achievable under the agreed network conditions. Conduct latency analysis across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services to confirm that no single module violates the budget. Document assumptions about network latency, jitter, and packet loss.

Establish a verification plan for SafetyAlert interface: define test scenarios that validate latency monitoring accuracy, threshold detection, and alert publication latency. Ensure that safety-critical alerts are delivered within a sub-latency-budget window (e.g., < 50 ms).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence. Verification feasibility is high, allocation clarity is excellent, and the composite DP is necessary and sufficient. However, the recommended revisions reveal critical dependencies and interface contracts that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure safety-critical latency guarantees are achievable and verifiable. Specifically: (1) DP1 (Latency Budgeting Discipline) requires explicit sub-FRs for budget allocation and enforcement across module boundaries; (2) SafetyAlert interface contract must be formalized as a sub-FR to prevent diffusion of DP5 logic into Safety Services; (3) Network Link capability advertisement (DP3 coordination) must be decomposed into a sub-FR to ensure QoS negotiation is feasible; (4) DP2 realization assumptions (physical vs. logical dual channels) must be validated through sub-FRs that clarify hardware constraints. Given high safety criticality, decomposition is necessary to make latency observability, safety guardrails, and interface contracts explicit and testable at lower levels.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Allocate end-to-end latency budget across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services modules such that each module's contribution respects the p95 ≤ 150 ms constraint and enforcement points are defined at Tasking ingress, Telemetry egress, and SafetyAlert publication.

FR1.2: Establish and publish SafetyAlert interface contract specifying metric types (latency, QoS status), update frequency, reliability guarantees (at-least-once delivery), and latency thresholds that trigger safety alerts, ensuring Safety Services can make failsafe decisions without requiring access to Teleoperation's internal monitoring logic.

FR1.3: Implement dual-channel architecture (control and data separation) with explicit realization assumptions documented: specify whether channels are physical (separate network interfaces) or logical (traffic prioritization on single link), and validate that chosen realization achieves latency budget under agreed network conditions.

FR1.4: Enable Network Link capability advertisement mechanism whereby Vehicle communicates max sampling rate, available bandwidth, and QoS constraints to Teleoperation, allowing adaptive QoS negotiation (DP3) to operate within feasible bounds without oscillation or failure.

FR1.5: Provide real-time latency observability interface (derived from Telemetry) that Teleoperation publishes for Safety Services consumption, enabling safety-critical latency awareness while preserving DP5 ownership and preventing circular dependencies.

FR1.6: Verify p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is achievable through latency analysis across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services, documenting assumptions about network latency, jitter, packet loss, and confirming no single module violates the allocated budget.

revise.why: DP1 (Latency Budgeting Discipline) must be clarified as a cross-cutting design constraint with explicit enforcement points, not a contained DP. Revise DP statement to: 'DP1: Enforce latency budgeting discipline across module boundaries via budget allocation, ingress/egress enforcement at Tasking/Telemetry interfaces, and SafetyAlert publication latency guarantees.' This revision makes DP1 actionable and verifiable at sub-FR level. Additionally, DP2 realization assumptions (physical vs. logical dual channels) must be explicitly documented in the DP statement to clarify hardware dependencies and enable feasibility validation.

assumptions: Network Link interface supports dual-channel separation (physical or logical); if unavailable, latency budget feasibility must be re-evaluated.
Vehicle can communicate capability constraints (max sampling rate, bandwidth, QoS limits) to Teleoperation via Network Link interface.

SafetyAlert interface can be formalized as a contract with explicit metric types, update frequency, and reliability guarantees without requiring Safety Services to access Teleoperation's internal monitoring logic.
Latency budget allocation across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services is feasible under agreed network conditions (latency, jitter, packet loss).
Teleoperation owns latency observability and monitoring (DP5); Safety Services consumes observability via SafetyAlert interface, not by accessing Teleoperation internals.
p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is achievable with current hardware and network assumptions; if not, FR or DP must be revised.
open_risks: DP1 (Latency Budgeting Discipline) diffusion: Budget enforcement logic could be duplicated in Vehicle or Safety Services if allocation and enforcement points are not explicitly defined. Mitigation: Sub-FR1.1 must specify enforcement points and ownership.

DP5 (Latency Monitoring) diffusion into Safety Services: If Safety Services requires sub-millisecond latency metrics for failsafe decisions, Teleoperation's monitoring subsystem must guarantee synchronous publication. Risk: Latency monitoring logic could leak into Safety Services if SafetyAlert contract is not clearly defined. Mitigation: Sub-FR1.2 must formalize SafetyAlert contract.

DP3 (Adaptive QoS) oscillation: QoS adaptation could fail if Vehicle-side resource constraints are not communicated to Teleoperation. Mitigation: Sub-FR1.4 must establish capability advertisement mechanism.

DP2 (Dual-Channel Architecture) hardware dependency: If hardware does not support physical dual channels, latency budget feasibility is at risk. Mitigation: Sub-FR1.3 must validate realization assumptions and document fallback options.

SafetyAlert latency: If SafetyAlert publication latency exceeds sub-budget window (e.g., > 50 ms), safety guardrails may fail. Mitigation: Sub-FR1.2 must specify SafetyAlert publication latency guarantee; Sub-FR1.6 must verify feasibility.
evidence_needed: Latency budget allocation table: Teleoperation, Vehicle, Safety Services contributions to p95 ≤ 150 ms, with enforcement points and ownership documented.
SafetyAlert interface specification: metric types, update frequency, reliability guarantees, latency thresholds, and contract validation test cases.
DP2 realization document: physical vs. logical dual-channel assumptions, hardware constraints, and feasibility analysis under agreed network conditions.
Network Link capability advertisement specification: data model for Vehicle to communicate max sampling rate, bandwidth, QoS constraints; Teleoperation negotiation logic.
Latency observability interface specification: metric types, publication frequency, consumer (Safety Services) requirements, and interface contract.
End-to-end latency analysis: simulation or measurement data confirming p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions (latency, jitter, packet loss); per-module contribution analysis.
SafetyAlert verification plan: test scenarios validating latency monitoring accuracy, threshold detection, alert publication latency (< 50 ms target), and failsafe behavior.
